summary:
The Library of Alexandria (c. 283–48 BC as a great institution) was the largest repository of written knowledge in the ancient world, established in [[event:1034|Foundation of Alexandria]] by the Ptolemies as part of their cultural program to make their capital the intellectual center of the Mediterranean. At its height, the library reportedly held hundreds of thousands of papyrus scrolls — estimates range from 400,000 to 700,000 — representing the accumulated literature, philosophy, science, and technical knowledge of the ancient world in Greek and in translation.

The library was associated with the Museum (Mouseion), an institution that provided stipends to scholars in residence — a model for the modern research university. Among those associated with the library were Euclid (geometry), [[event:1199|Eratosthenes Measures Earth]] (geography and astronomy), [[event:1201|Hipparchus Star Catalog]] (astronomy), Callimachus (bibliography), and Apollonius of Rhodes (epic poetry). The library's staff systematically collected texts from across the Hellenistic world, reportedly requiring ships docking at Alexandria to surrender any books for copying before receiving them back.

The famous 'burning of the Library of Alexandria' is largely a myth generated by the desire for a single dramatic catastrophic explanation. Julius Caesar accidentally burned a warehouse of books near the harbor in 48 BC, which ancient sources record; Caliph Omar's conquest in 641 AD has been blamed since medieval times without ancient attestation. In reality, the library declined gradually over centuries through neglect, budget cuts, the removal of books by various rulers, and the conversion of Alexandria from a pagan to Christian intellectual culture. Its symbolic importance as a monument to the ambition to collect all human knowledge has made it a lasting cultural reference point for the fragility of civilization's intellectual heritage.
